points.headline: Microsoft Build 2026 goes all-in on agents and its own models.
points.body: Microsoft unveiled seven in-house MAI models, led by the 1-trillion-parameter MAI-Thinking-1 reasoning model and a Copilot-tuned MAI-Code-1-Flash, loosening its dependence on OpenAI. It also launched Scout, an always-on personal assistant inside Microsoft 365, and Project Solara, an Android variant built for AI agents instead of apps.

points.headline: Trump signs a watered-down AI executive order.
points.body: After industry pushback, the president scrapped a tougher draft and signed an order creating only a voluntary framework for AI labs to share frontier models with the government before release. Policy experts warn the "trusted partner" access could still let officials pick winners and losers.

points.headline: OpenAI pushes Codex from coding tool to everyday work platform.
points.body: OpenAI rolled out role-specific Codex plugins for analysts, marketers, designers, investors and bankers, plus a "Sites" web-hosting feature and inline "Annotations," reframing Codex as an operating environment for knowledge work. In parallel, GPT-5.5, GPT-5.4 and Codex became generally available on Amazon Bedrock.

points.headline: Anthropic files for what could be the largest IPO ever.
points.body: The maker of Claude confidentially submitted IPO paperwork to the SEC, weeks after topping OpenAI's valuation and just after SpaceX's own IPO announcement. The milestone was punctuated by a Claude outage on the very day of the float news.

points.headline: The cost of running AI starts to bite.
points.body: GitHub Copilot's switch to usage-based pricing triggered a revolt, with some developers burning a month's credits in a single day and threatening to leave, while Uber capped employee AI spending after blowing its budget in four months. Vendors are responding with local-first hardware like Microsoft's Surface RTX Spark Dev Box and Perplexity's hybrid local-cloud inference to dodge per-token cloud costs.

points.headline: AI agents become the new attack surface.
points.body: Hackers simply asked Meta's AI support chatbot to hand over high-profile Instagram accounts, and it worked, while researchers disclosed "BadHost," a Starlette auth-bypass flaw exposing AI agent infrastructure behind 325 million weekly downloads. Microsoft answered with MXC, an OS-level sandbox for agents already backed by OpenAI and Nvidia.
